sourceDesc.p: Downloaded from https://medium.com/@louBurnard/the-black-and-white-challenge-and-me-5f0a2f4780ae and translated from HTML to TEI

div.head: The Black-and-white Challenge and Me

figure.head: Day 7: road sign at the junction of Lonsdale Road and National Cycle Route 51 south

div.p: An operational definition says that a Facebook “Challenge” is when you do (and record) something or other vaguely unusual but recordable on Facebook, and then nominate one of your friends to do likewise, usually repeatedly over a period of time such as a week. The “something or other” might take a spectacular form such as “film yourself emptying a bucket of cold water over your own head” or a more artistic one such as the “black-and-white challenge” , in which you are just required to post a monochrome photo innocent of people, pets, or explanation. There are doubtless many others, but these two seem to have been taken up by enough otherwise unconnected people to have achieved the dubious status of being an “internet meme”: a phenomenon that takes on a life of its own as different members of the social network pick it up, or notice others picking it up. This posting is me musing on my experience of the Black and White Challenge, which I completed yesterday, coincidentally on my birthday.

I first noticed this challenge a couple of months ago, and was challenged myself just last week. (Full disclosure, I asked a daughter — who had herself been challenged — to challenge me). I take a lot of photos, but usually not in black and white, and I liked the idea of being constrained to do something a bit different, within a predefined arbitrary framework. It’s the Oulipian in me. And taking black and white photos is quite a different exercise: you have only shapes, light, and shade to play with, and you have to pick and choose your subject matter. Which is probably why some people who take (or are forced into) this challenge tend to fall back on the same rather restricted set of abstract patterns, bare trees, architectural panoramas, close-ups of domestic interiors, etc. A Slate hack recently got very snooty about this pointing out that The prompt to eschew color, people, and explanatory text has given participants free rein to post cringe-worthy “arty” pictures they’d normally have the good sense to be embarrassed by: their shadow-dipped lattes, their brooding pets, their kids’ tilted-over toys, often framed diagonally to add that extra “I’m doing serious photography” edge…but this seems to miss the point: you can’t claim that the challenge is too simple to be a challenge, and the same time complain that people aren’t finding it challenging enough. Going monochrome requires you to see the world in a different way, and it’s not always easy. The same Google search that threw up the above source (trust me: I do not habitually browse Slate) also threw up several bloggers who wanted to showcase their results, and were clearly relishing this opportunity to deploy a little creative ingenuity. As you might expect, some of them were dire, but some were quite impressive . And I am quite pleased with my own efforts too (see below).

figure.head: Winter sunshine and shadows outside Joe’s restaurant in Summertown. Too cold for anyone but desperate smokers though.

div.p: But the aspect of this challenge that I wasn’t really expecting, or at least hadn’t thought about much, was the way other people reacted to being nominated. I decided I would constrain my nominations too, choosing people I now see only on Facebook and who have not to my knowledge been challenged in this way before. I alternated genders and aimed for a wide geography. Seven choices doesn’t give an opportunity to do much more in the name of diversity. Consequently, none of my nominations aimed at close friends or family members (the latter because they were all busy challenging each other).

My first nominee flatly refused to participate. My second politely declined. My third said he might get round to it. My fourth, bless her, said she’d give it a shot, and has now started. My fifth agreed to do part of the challenge — the artistic part, but not the social one. My sixth and seventh are (to date) silent. Does this reluctance say something about people in general, or about the specific ones I have contacted ? Is it a typical take-up rate ? Of course if Facebook offered a decent API (and I was clever enough to use it), these are questions I could answer automatically. But just for the record, let’s manually compute the following magical number as a measure of the success of any one set of challenge outcomes : the number of photos delivered + the number of photos delivered by challengees + the number of persons challenged who both delivered at least one photo and challenged one further person. We can’t sit around waiting forever to calculate this number, but today, eight days after I took up the challenge, my magical number is 7+ 4 + 1 or a paltry 12. Whereas the person who challenged me seems to have clocked up a stunning 6 + 21 + 3, a magnificent 30. I am standing by all agog for news of other people’s scores.
In conclusion, for the benefit of non-facebookers, I’m including here carefully curated copies of my seven monochrome moments. I still like all of them. Let me know if you do too.

figure.head: Day 1 : Oyster mushrooms on sale at the Summertown Farmers market
Day 2 : First snow fall of the year hits our back garden in La Vergne. The Dr Seuss ladder on the shed is still intact, but only just.
Day 3 : Experimenting with my phone’s ability to mimic a real camera: this is in front of the church currently being renovated at Place d’Estienne d’Orves, Paris
Day 4: An apple from my French garden with a rather saucy posterior
Day 5 : Paperwork piled up on my Oxford desk. Mostly the stuff of unfiled expense claims.
